places.name: La Petite Maison
places.reviewSummary: The most famous table in Nice, serving polished Niçoise-Provençal classics in a buzzy bistro room near the Opera. The original of a now-global mini empire.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the tomatoes, black truffle pasta and lively scene, though many flag steep prices and tight tables.
places.localTip: Order family-style and start with the heirloom tomato salad and the whole sea bass to share.
places.story: La Petite Maison began as a humble grocer's corner before chef Nicole Rubi turned it into the riviera's most name-dropped dining room, where film stars and footballers crowd in beside locals. The kitchen worships the Niçoise pantry: sun-blasted tomatoes, olive oil, anchovy and basil. It's where Nice shows off without ever leaving its roots.
places.highlight: Don't miss the legendary heirloom tomato salad.

places.name: Chez Pipo
places.reviewSummary: A century-old institution near the port baking socca, the chickpea-flour pancake that is Nice's defining street food. Smoky, blistered and addictive.
places.googleReviewSummary: Beloved for crisp, oven-fresh socca and cheap pichets of rosé, though reviewers warn of queues and brisk, no-frills service.
places.localTip: Socca is only good fresh from the wood oven, so eat it the moment it lands on your table.
places.story: Chez Pipo has been turning out socca near the port since 1923, the same chickpea batter ladled onto vast copper pans and slid into a roaring wood oven. Old Niçois say the smoke and the salt are the secret, and you'll smell it before you see the door. It's the city's working-class soul on a paper plate.
places.highlight: Don't miss it scattered with black pepper and washed down with rosé.

places.name: La Merenda
places.reviewSummary: A pocket-sized temple of authentic Niçoise cooking run by a former Michelin-starred chef who walked away from the stars. No phone, no cards.
places.googleReviewSummary: Diners praise the soul-deep stockfish, daube and pasta, while noting cramped stools, cash-only rules and walk-up-only booking.
places.localTip: There's no telephone; walk past in the morning to book your table in person for that evening.
places.story: Dominique Le Stanc gave up a two-star kitchen at the Negresco to cook market food at this dozen-seat counter, and Nice loved him for it. The menu is chalked on a board: tripe, stockfish, pissaladière, exactly as grandmothers made it. Eating here feels like being let in on the city's best-kept secret.
places.highlight: Don't miss the sautéed courgette flowers.

places.name: Peixes
places.reviewSummary: A bright, modern seafood bar serving ceviche, tartare and grilled catch with a Mediterranean-meets-Latin tilt. A local favourite for fresh fish done lightly.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers rave about the ceviche and natural wines, though some say tables are tight and it gets loud at peak.
places.localTip: Go at lunch for the same fish at gentler prices, and ask what came off the boat that morning.
places.story: Peixes helped spark Nice's new wave of young, sea-driven kitchens, swapping heavy sauces for citrus, chilli and raw fish glittering with olive oil. The name simply means 'fishes', and the chalkboard changes with the day's catch. It's proof the Riviera can feel effortlessly contemporary.

places.name: Lou Pilha Leva
places.reviewSummary: A bustling corner counter in the old town serving socca, pissaladière, farcis and beignets at long communal benches. Pure cheap-and-local energy.
places.googleReviewSummary: Loved for variety and value, though reviewers note crowds, shared tables and uneven service at busy times.
places.localTip: Order a sampler of socca, a slice of pissaladière and stuffed vegetables to taste the whole Niçoise repertoire.
places.story: Lou Pilha Leva means roughly 'take it and go' in the old Niçard tongue, and that's the spirit: order at the hatch, grab a bench, eat with your elbows out. It sits where two old-town lanes collide, perfumed by onion tart and frying chickpea batter. It's the cheapest, friendliest crash course in Niçoise food going.
places.highlight: Don't miss the pissaladière with caramelised onions and anchovy.

places.name: Olive et Artichaut
places.reviewSummary: A warm, slate-walled bistro turning daily market produce into refined Provençal plates. A reliably excellent old-town dinner.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers consistently praise inventive seasonal cooking and value, with the only caveat being its small, often-booked dining room.
places.localTip: Reserve ahead and trust the short market menu rather than ordering off-piste.
places.story: Named for two pillars of the Niçoise larder, Olive et Artichaut sources from the Cours Saleya market each morning and lets the season write the menu. The room is tiny and candlelit, the cooking precise but generous. It's where in-the-know visitors go when they want old-town charm with a modern kitchen behind it.

places.name: Bistrot d'Antoine
places.reviewSummary: A classic Lyon-meets-Nice bistro from chef Armand Crespo, chalkboard menu heavy with bistronomy comfort. A Michelin Bib Gourmand stalwart.
places.googleReviewSummary: Diners praise hearty, well-priced French classics and warm service, though the snug room books out fast.
places.localTip: Book a few days ahead and save room for the daily dessert specials.
places.story: Bistrot d'Antoine has long been the address Niçois recommend when friends visit and want 'a proper bistro'. The menu swings between Provençal and old-school French, all written by hand and changed daily. Decades on, the bentwood chairs and butcher-paper tablecloths still feel exactly right.

places.name: Bistrot Gourmand Comptoir du Marché
places.reviewSummary: A snug, brick-walled bistro a step off Cours Saleya, blending Niçoise and Italian influences with daily blackboard specials.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the friendly atmosphere and fresh market plates, though they note limited seating and the need to book.
places.localTip: Sit at the bar if the tables are full; the daily fish is usually the smart order.
places.story: Tucked on tiny Rue du Marché, this comptoir leans into Nice's deep Italian roots, just twenty miles from the border. Plates of fresh pasta and Mediterranean fish arrive without fuss in a room barely wider than its bar. It captures the easy cross-border cooking that makes the city's food unique.

places.name: Café Marché by Emi
places.reviewSummary: A bright speciality-coffee spot pulling carefully sourced espresso and pour-overs in the smart Carré d'Or. A go-to for serious caffeine.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers praise the coffee quality and brunch plates, with a few noting it's compact and busy on weekends.
places.localTip: Come on a weekday morning for a calmer room and the freshest pastries.
places.story: As Nice's coffee scene matured beyond corner cafés, spots like this brought roast-forward espresso and flat whites to a city raised on quick comptoir coffees. The vibe is light, plant-dotted and friendly. It's where the new Nice slows down for a properly made cup.

places.name: Maison Auer
places.reviewSummary: A dazzling 1820 chocolate and confectionery house opposite the Opera, famed for candied fruits and crystallised citrus. A living museum of sweets.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors adore the gilded Belle Époque interior and candied fruits, though some find the artisan prices high.
places.localTip: Buy a box of fruits confits, a regional speciality, and admire the original 19th-century décor as you queue.
places.story: Maison Auer has glittered across from the Opera since 1820, its mirrored, chandeliered salon run by the same family for generations. They candy local clementines and figs the slow old way and were even supplier to Queen Victoria. Stepping inside is a sugar-dusted trip straight into the Belle Époque.
places.highlight: Don't miss the candied citrus and chocolate-dipped orange peel.

places.name: Fenocchio
places.reviewSummary: The legendary ice-cream parlour on Place Rossetti with nearly a hundred flavours, from black olive to tomato-basil. An old-town rite of passage.
places.googleReviewSummary: Adored for the wild flavour range and fountain-side seating, though reviewers warn of long lines and brisk service.
places.localTip: Be brave with the unusual flavours like fig, lavender or tomato-basil; they're the reason people return.
places.story: Fenocchio has scooped on Place Rossetti since 1966, growing into a flavour laboratory where black olive and Provençal rose sit beside chocolate and pistachio. Locals debate their favourites the way others argue football. Eating a cone by the square's splashing fountain is one of Nice's simplest pleasures.
places.highlight: Don't miss tasting one savoury flavour alongside a classic.

places.name: Malaussèna Coffee
places.reviewSummary: A relaxed neighbourhood café near the port doing speciality coffee, brunch bowls and homemade cakes. A favourite local hang.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the friendly team and generous brunch, with the main caveat being weekend waits for a table.
places.localTip: Pair a flat white with the cake of the day and grab a sunny pavement seat.
places.story: Away from the tourist crush, the streets behind the port have become Nice's casual brunch belt, and cafés like this anchor them. Expect avocado plates, good beans and a neighbourly buzz of regulars. It's the everyday Nice that visitors rarely see but always enjoy.

places.name: Café de Turin
places.reviewSummary: A storied 1908 brasserie under the arcades of Place Garibaldi, famous citywide for ice-cold oysters and shellfish platters. A Nice institution.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers praise the fresh seafood and historic setting, though some mention crowds and uneven service at peak hours.
places.localTip: Sit under the arcade, order a dozen oysters and a glass of white, and watch Place Garibaldi go by.
places.story: Café de Turin has shucked oysters beneath Place Garibaldi's ochre arcades since 1908, when the square marked the gateway to Italy. Generations of Niçois have started celebrations over its seafood towers. The marble, the mirrors and the briny platters make it feel pleasingly unchanged.
places.highlight: Don't miss the platter of oysters and sea urchins in season.

places.name: LAC Patisserie (Pâtisserie LAC)
places.reviewSummary: An elegant pastry boutique and salon by acclaimed pâtissier Pascal Lac, with jewel-like cakes and chocolates. A sweet stop in the smart quarter.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers rave about the refined pastries and chocolates, with a few noting premium prices and limited seating.
places.localTip: Take a couple of individual pastries to eat by the sea, or sit in for a proper afternoon tea.
places.story: Pascal Lac earned a national reputation crafting precise, glossy pastries, and his Carré d'Or boutique shows it off. Each cake is a small architectural feat in chocolate and fruit. It's the polished, patisserie-loving side of a city that takes its sweets seriously.

places.name: Promenade des Anglais
places.reviewSummary: The sweeping seven-kilometre seafront boulevard along the Baie des Anges, lined with palms, blue chairs and Belle Époque hotels. Nice's defining image.
places.googleReviewSummary: Universally loved for sea views, strolling and people-watching, though reviewers note heavy summer crowds and pebble beaches.
places.localTip: Walk or cycle it at sunset, then claim one of the iconic blue chairs to watch the light fade.
places.story: The Promenade des Anglais was funded by Nice's wintering English colony in the 1820s, who wanted a smooth path along the shingle. Today its palms, mosaics and azure chairs are the postcard everyone knows. Skating, cycling and simply ambling here is how the whole city unwinds.
places.highlight: Don't miss the view back to the old town from the pink-domed Negresco.

places.name: Colline du Château (Castle Hill)
places.reviewSummary: A green hilltop park crowning the old town, with a waterfall, ruins and the postcard panorama over the Baie des Anges. The best view in Nice.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the sweeping vistas and shade, though some mention the climb and that the actual castle is long gone.
places.localTip: Take the free lift by the Tour Bellanda up, then wander down through the old town on foot.
places.story: Castle Hill once held the medieval citadel that gave Nice its name, until Louis XIV's army demolished it in 1706. What remains is a fragrant park of pines and a thundering man-made waterfall. From the top, the curving bay and terracotta rooftops unfold like a living postcard.
places.highlight: Don't miss the panoramic terrace over the bay and old town.

places.name: Cours Saleya Market
places.reviewSummary: The vibrant heart of the old town, a flower and produce market by day and antiques on Mondays, ringed by terraces. Nice's social and culinary hub.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the colours, flowers and produce, though some find restaurant prices on the square touristy.
places.localTip: Come before 11am for the liveliest market, and buy socca or pissaladière from a stall rather than a sit-down terrace.
places.story: Cours Saleya has been Nice's marketplace for centuries, its flower stalls so famous that Matisse painted the blooms from a window above. Tuesday to Sunday it overflows with produce, herbs and lavender; Mondays it turns to antiques. It is, quite simply, where the old town comes alive.
places.highlight: Don't miss the dawn flower market and a slice of warm socca.

places.name: Place Masséna
places.reviewSummary: The grand red-ochre central square with checkerboard paving, fountains and the gleaming statues that light up at night. The city's symbolic heart.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the striking architecture and night-time light statues, though it can be busy with trams and crowds.
places.localTip: Return after dark to see the Conversation à Nice statues glowing atop their poles above the square.
places.story: Laid out in the 1840s and framed by arcaded red façades, Place Masséna is where Nice gathers for carnival, markets and New Year. The Apollo fountain and the seven seated figures perched on poles, lit in shifting colours, give it real drama. Trams glide silently across its checkerboard stone all day.
places.highlight: Don't miss the square at night when the statues glow.

places.name: Cathédrale Sainte-Réparate
places.reviewSummary: Nice's 17th-century Baroque cathedral on pretty Place Rossetti, with a glazed-tile dome and an opulent gilded interior. The spiritual heart of the old town.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers admire the ornate Baroque interior and tiled dome, with a few noting it's compact and busy with tourists.
places.localTip: Step inside for a cool, quiet moment, then take a Fenocchio ice cream on the square outside.
places.story: Dedicated to Nice's young patron saint Réparate, whose body legend says drifted here on a flower-strewn boat, the cathedral rose in the 1650s in full Genoese Baroque. Its tiled dome echoes the city's Italian past. Inside, marble and gilt glow beneath candlelight on the old town's prettiest square.

places.name: Cathédrale Orthodoxe Russe Saint-Nicolas
places.reviewSummary: A jaw-dropping Russian Orthodox cathedral with six colourful onion domes, the largest of its kind outside Russia. A surprising slice of Tsarist Nice.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers are wowed by the domes and iconostasis, though some note dress-code rules and that interior photos are restricted.
places.localTip: Dress modestly with shoulders covered, and visit on the way to or from the train station, a short walk away.
places.story: Built in 1912 and funded by Tsar Nicholas II, this cathedral recalls the era when Russian aristocrats wintered on the Riviera in great numbers. Its candy-coloured onion domes rise startlingly above the palms. Inside, gilded icons and incense transport you a thousand miles east.
places.highlight: Don't miss the richly painted iconostasis inside.

places.name: Hôtel Negresco
places.reviewSummary: The pink-domed 1913 palace hotel on the Promenade, stuffed with art and a glittering Baccarat chandelier. Nice's most iconic building.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the opulent lobby and history, though some find the grandeur and prices intimidating.
places.localTip: You don't need a room; step into the lobby to see the art, or have a drink in Le Relais bar.
places.story: The Negresco opened in 1913 as the dream of a Romanian hotelier and instantly became the address of kings and film stars. Beneath its rose-coloured dome hangs a Baccarat chandelier of 16,000 crystals, twin to one in the Kremlin. Today the corridors double as a private museum of paintings and antiques.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Salon Royal and its colossal chandelier.

places.name: Cimiez Roman Ruins & Arena
places.address: 184 Avenue des Arènes de Cimiez, 06000 Nice
places.reviewSummary: The remains of the Roman town of Cemenelum, with an amphitheatre, baths and an olive-grove park beside the Matisse Museum. Ancient Nice in the hills.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers enjoy the peaceful ruins and shaded grove, though some note signage is sparse and it's a tram-plus-walk from the centre.
places.localTip: Combine it with the Matisse and Archaeology museums next door, and picnic among the old olive trees.
places.story: Long before the Promenade, Romans built Cemenelum on the Cimiez hill, complete with arena and grand baths. The olive grove that now shades the ruins hosts the famous Nice Jazz Festival's spirit each summer. It's a serene, leafy counterpoint to the busy seafront below.

places.name: Marché de la Libération
places.address: Avenue Malausséna / Place du Général de Gaulle, 06000 Nice
places.reviewSummary: The big, no-nonsense local market north of the centre where Niçois actually shop, with fish, produce and a buzzing fishmongers' row. Authentic and unpolished.
places.googleReviewSummary: Locals praise the freshness, prices and atmosphere, while visitors note it's less picturesque and closes by early afternoon.
places.localTip: Arrive before noon and grab a coffee from a stall to watch the fish auction-style banter.
places.story: While tourists flock to Cours Saleya, Niçois do their real shopping at the Libération market around the Gambetta statue. The poissonnerie aisle is the city's most colourful, all glistening Mediterranean catch and shouted prices. It's where you feel how Nice eats when no one's watching.

places.name: Plage de la Réserve / Coco Beach
places.reviewSummary: A rocky swimming cove east of the port, beloved by locals for clear water, ladders into the sea and far fewer crowds. A real Niçois bathing spot.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the clean water and local feel, though they warn it's rocky, has limited shade and can get choppy.
places.localTip: Bring water shoes and a mat; there's no sand, but the swimming is the best in the city.
places.story: Past the port, where the coast turns wild beneath Mont Boron, locals slip into the sea from concrete platforms and rocks at Coco Beach and La Réserve. There are no parasol rows, just clear water and divers. It's the Nice that residents guard for themselves.
places.highlight: Don't miss the sunset swim with the city glowing behind you.

places.name: Place du Pin / Le Port quarter
places.address: Place de l'Île de Beauté, 06300 Nice
places.reviewSummary: The colourful working harbour ringed by antique shops, neo-classical churches and trendy bistros. Nice's increasingly hip portside neighbourhood.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers like the photogenic boats and growing restaurant scene, though parking and a slight walk from the centre come up.
places.localTip: Come for dinner around the port, then browse the antiques shops on Rue Antoine Gautier by day.
places.story: Nice's port, dug in the 18th century, is framed by Sardinian-era façades in ochre and pink and watched over by the twin-towered church of Notre-Dame du Port. Once gritty, it's now one of the city's most fashionable quarters. Yachts and fishing boats still share the same blue water.

places.name: Parc du Mont Boron
places.address: Route Forestière du Mont Boron, 06300 Nice
places.reviewSummary: A pine-forested hill east of the city laced with walking trails and views over Nice, Villefranche and Cap Ferrat. A favourite local escape.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the trails and panoramas, though some note it's a climb and best reached by car or a steep walk.
places.localTip: Follow the path to the 19th-century Fort du Mont Alban for the widest double-bay view.
places.story: Mont Boron's forested slopes have drawn walkers since the Belle Époque, when villas of the rich climbed its flanks. From the ridge you can see Nice's bay on one side and Villefranche's on the other. It's where Niçois come to breathe, jog and watch the sea change colour.
places.highlight: Don't miss the panorama over Villefranche-sur-Mer.

places.name: Rue Droite & Place Rossetti aperitif crawl
places.reviewSummary: The atmospheric spine of the old town, threading past palaces, artisan shops and tiny bars perfect for an early-evening drink. Pure Vieux Nice.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the medieval lanes and local bars, though the narrow streets get busy on summer evenings.
places.localTip: Do a slow apéro crawl from bar to bar rather than committing to one terrace.
places.story: Rue Droite cuts dead straight through the heart of old Nice, lined with Genoese palaces, bakeries and the studios of artisans. As dusk falls, locals spill from doorway bars with glasses of rosé and pastis. Drifting from one to the next is the quintessential Niçois evening.

places.name: Espuno Boulangerie
places.reviewSummary: A respected old-town bakery turning out award-winning bread plus Niçoise specialities like pissaladière and tourte de blettes. A neighbourhood staple.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers praise the quality bread and local tarts, with the only gripe being a small shop and lunchtime queues.
places.localTip: Try the tourte de blettes, a sweet-savoury chard tart that's a true Niçoise oddity.
places.story: Espuno has earned its reputation the hard way, with bakers winning awards for their baguettes while keeping the old Niçoise tarts alive. The tourte de blettes, made from Swiss chard, raisins and pine nuts, surprises everyone. It's the kind of bakery that quietly keeps a city's food memory intact.

places.name: Èze Village Day Trip
places.reviewSummary: A medieval eagle's-nest village clinging to a cliff above the sea, with a cactus garden at the summit and dizzying Riviera views. An easy half-day from Nice.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers are enchanted by the lanes and panoramas, though they warn of steep climbs and tour-bus crowds at midday.
places.localTip: Take the cheap bus from Nice and arrive early or late to beat the coach groups.
places.story: Perched 400 metres above the Mediterranean, Èze's stone lanes spiral up to an exotic garden where you can see all the way to Corsica on a clear day. Walt Disney and countless artists fell for it. From Nice it's a short, spectacular ride along the Riviera's most dramatic coast.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Jardin Exotique at the village's summit.

places.name: Villefranche-sur-Mer
places.reviewSummary: A jewel-box harbour town one stop from Nice, with a deep-blue bay, a citadel and a frescoed chapel by Jean Cocteau. The Riviera at its prettiest.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers adore the colourful waterfront and swimming, though parking is tight and beaches fill up fast in summer.
places.localTip: Take the six-minute train from Nice and swim off the sheltered town beach before lunch on the quay.
places.story: Villefranche curves around one of the deepest natural harbours in the Med, so blue it once anchored the US Sixth Fleet. Jean Cocteau decorated its little fishermen's chapel with dreamlike frescoes. A six-minute train ride from Nice drops you into a postcard.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Cocteau-painted Chapelle Saint-Pierre.

places.name: Sea Kayaking off Nice / Villefranche Bay
places.reviewSummary: Guided sea-kayak and paddle outings from the port along the coast to hidden coves and the Villefranche bay. Nice from the water.
places.googleReviewSummary: Paddlers praise the clear water and friendly guides, though trips depend on calm sea conditions.
places.localTip: Book a morning slot when the sea is glassy and the light on the cliffs is best.
places.story: Seeing Nice from a kayak flips the whole city around: the pastel old town and Castle Hill rise behind you as you skim past coves no road reaches. Guides paddle toward Villefranche's sheltered bay, stopping to swim. It's the most refreshing way to grasp the coastline's drama.

places.name: Villa Ephrussi de Rothschild
places.reviewSummary: A rose-pink Italianate palace on Cap Ferrat surrounded by nine themed gardens and dancing musical fountains. A Belle Époque fantasy near Nice.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers are dazzled by the gardens and sea views, though some find it pricey and busy on summer afternoons.
places.localTip: Time your visit for the musical fountain shows, which run on the hour, and bring time for all nine gardens.
places.story: Baroness Béatrice de Rothschild built this confection in 1912 to display her treasures and her gardens, which she designed to resemble a ship's deck floating between two bays. Pink marble, tapestries and fountains abound. It's perhaps the most romantic house on the entire Riviera.
places.highlight: Don't miss the French garden's musical fountains.

places.name: Les Petits Farcis Cooking Class
places.address: Rue du Marché area, Vieux Nice, 06300 Nice
places.reviewSummary: A hands-on Niçoise cooking class that starts with a market tour and ends cooking socca, petits farcis and pissaladière. A delicious deep dive into local food.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests rave about the market knowledge and recipes, with the only caveat being it books out weeks ahead.
places.localTip: Book well in advance and come hungry; you'll shop, cook and feast across a whole morning.
places.story: Food writer Rosa Jackson runs these classes from an old-town kitchen, beginning each morning among the Cours Saleya stalls explaining chard, anchovy and olive oil. Then you cook the true Niçoise repertoire and sit down to eat it. Few experiences teach the city's soul so deliciously.

places.name: Grasse Perfume Day Trip
places.reviewSummary: The world's perfume capital in the hills behind Nice, with historic distilleries offering free tours and make-your-own-scent workshops. A fragrant excursion.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers enjoy the perfume history and workshops, though some find the town itself sleepy and the journey long by bus.
places.localTip: Pre-book a 'create your own perfume' workshop at one of the historic houses for the most memorable visit.
places.story: Grasse has distilled jasmine, rose and orange blossom into the world's finest perfumes since the 1600s, supplying the great Paris houses. Its old distilleries still welcome visitors with copper stills and dizzying scent organs. An hour from Nice, it's where the Riviera's flowers become liquid gold.

places.name: Musée Matisse
places.address: 164 Avenue des Arènes de Cimiez, 06000 Nice
places.reviewSummary: A red-ochre Genoese villa in the Cimiez olive grove holding one of the world's great Matisse collections. The artist's adopted city honours him.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the intimate collection and setting, though some say it's smaller than expected and a tram ride from the centre.
places.localTip: Pair it with the Roman ruins and Archaeology Museum in the same park for a full Cimiez morning.
places.story: Henri Matisse spent his last decades in Nice, dazzled by its light, and chose to be buried in Cimiez nearby. This villa gathers his paintings, drawings and famous paper cut-outs across his whole career. Surrounded by ancient olives, it's a serene shrine to the master of colour.
places.highlight: Don't miss the late paper cut-out works.

places.name: Musée National Marc Chagall
places.reviewSummary: A purpose-built museum housing Chagall's luminous Biblical Message cycle, his largest single body of religious work. A jewel of colour and dream.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers are moved by the vivid canvases and stained glass, with the main caveat being timed-entry queues in summer.
places.localTip: Sit in the auditorium beneath the blue stained-glass windows for a quiet, glowing pause.
places.story: Marc Chagall worked with the French state to build this museum himself in 1973, designing the spaces around his great Biblical Message paintings. Reds and blues swirl with floating lovers, goats and prophets. Few museums feel so completely shaped by a single artist's vision.
places.highlight: Don't miss the blue-glass concert hall.

places.name: MAMAC (Musée d'Art Moderne et d'Art Contemporain)
places.reviewSummary: Nice's bold contemporary museum, strong on the Nouveau Réalisme and Pop movements, with Yves Klein, Niki de Saint Phalle and Warhol. Don't miss the rooftop.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers praise the collection and free rooftop views, though some say the building's layout feels dated.
places.localTip: Ride to the rooftop terrace for a free panorama over the old town's rooftops to the sea.
places.story: MAMAC celebrates the School of Nice, the daring 1960s movement led by hometown hero Yves Klein and his patented blue. Niki de Saint Phalle's giant figures and Pop classics fill the galleries. Its rooftop, free to all, hands you one of the best city views going.
places.highlight: Don't miss the rooftop terrace at sunset.

places.name: Musée Masséna
places.address: 65 Rue de France / 35 Promenade des Anglais, 06000 Nice
places.reviewSummary: A sumptuous seafront villa museum charting Nice's Belle Époque, with gardens beside the Negresco. Lavish rooms and rich local history.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the opulent interiors and gardens, though some find the labelling limited in English.
places.localTip: The garden is free to enter and a lovely shaded pause right on the Promenade.
places.story: This Italianate palace was built in 1901 for a descendant of Napoleon's marshal André Masséna, a Nice native. Its gilded salons now tell the story of the city's golden age of aristocratic winters. The palm garden between it and the sea is one of the Promenade's hidden delights.

places.name: Musée des Beaux-Arts Jules Chéret
places.reviewSummary: Nice's fine-arts museum in a former Russian princess's villa, spanning Old Masters to Impressionism, with rooms devoted to poster artist Jules Chéret.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers enjoy the elegant villa and varied collection, though they note it's a quiet, lesser-known stop off the main drag.
places.localTip: It's rarely crowded; go when you want art in calm with the breeze through grand old rooms.
places.story: Housed in an 1878 villa built for a Ukrainian princess, this museum gathers Italian, French and Dutch paintings alongside Carpeaux sculptures. It celebrates Jules Chéret, the 'father of the modern poster', who died in Nice. It's a graceful, underrated escape into older art.

places.name: Galerie des Ponchettes
places.reviewSummary: A long, low former fishermen's arsenal on the seafront, now a free municipal gallery showing changing modern and photographic exhibitions.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers like the free entry and seafront setting, though shows are small and rotate, so quality varies.
places.localTip: Pop in while strolling the Quai des États-Unis; it's free and takes only twenty minutes.
places.story: The Ponchettes were 18th-century fishermen's stores built into the seafront arcades, later championed as a gallery by Matisse and Dufy. Today the white vaulted space hosts contemporary and photo exhibitions for free. It's an easy, atmospheric art stop between the beach and the old town.

places.name: Musée des Arts Asiatiques
places.reviewSummary: A serene Kenzō Tange-designed white pavilion floating on a lake in Phoenix Park, devoted to Asian art and tea ceremony. A tranquil architectural gem.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers praise the calm architecture and tea ceremonies, though it's near the airport and a tram ride from the centre.
places.localTip: Check ahead for the Japanese tea ceremony sessions held in the pavilion.
places.story: Star architect Kenzō Tange set this gleaming marble-and-glass museum on a lake beside the lush Parc Phoenix. Inside, Japanese, Chinese, Indian and Cambodian art is shown with great restraint. Designed around geometry and water, the building is as much the artwork as its contents.

places.name: Opéra de Nice
places.reviewSummary: Nice's gilded 19th-century opera house staging opera, ballet and symphony concerts. A red-and-gold Italianate jewel by the old town.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the ornate auditorium and acoustics, though some note tight legroom in the historic seating.
places.localTip: Even cheaper upper-tier seats give the full effect of the gilded ceiling; dress up a little.
places.story: Rebuilt in 1885 after a fire, the Opéra de Nice rose in opulent Italian style with a frescoed ceiling and chandeliers. Its stage has welcomed great singers for over a century. Catching a performance here is a chance to share in the Riviera's old high-society glamour.

places.name: Shapko Bar
places.reviewSummary: An intimate old-town bar with live jazz, funk and soul most nights of the week. The city's go-to for late-night live music.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers praise the talented house bands and atmosphere, though it gets packed and warm when busy.
places.localTip: Arrive before the band starts to grab a spot near the stage; sets run late into the night.
places.story: Shapko has become the beating heart of live music in old Nice, its small stage hosting jazz, blues and funk acts that draw a mixed crowd of locals and travellers. The room is snug, the playing serious. On a good night it feels like the whole old town is swaying inside.

places.name: Théâtre de Verdure
places.reviewSummary: An open-air amphitheatre in the seafront gardens hosting summer concerts and a key Nice Jazz Festival stage. Music under the stars.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the al fresco setting and lineups, though seating can be hard and sound varies with the wind.
places.localTip: Check the summer programme; many shows here are excellent value compared with indoor venues.
places.story: Set in the leafy Jardin Albert 1er steps from the sea, the Théâtre de Verdure has hosted open-air concerts for decades. Each summer it becomes a stage for the famed Nice Jazz Festival. Hearing music drift over the palm-fringed gardens on a warm night is a Riviera pleasure.
places.highlight: Don't miss a Nice Jazz Festival night in July.

places.name: Palais Nikaïa
places.reviewSummary: Nice's large modern concert hall hosting international pop, rock and comedy tours. The city's main big-name music venue.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers praise the acoustics and sightlines, though they note it's far from the centre with tricky post-show transport.
places.localTip: Plan your return trip in advance, as taxis and trams are mobbed after big shows.
places.story: Out near the airport, the Palais Nikaïa is where the biggest touring acts play when they reach the Riviera. Its sweeping roof and flexible floor handle everything from rock to ice shows. For a true arena night in Nice, this is the address.

places.name: Wayne's Bar
places.reviewSummary: A rowdy British-style pub in the old town with nightly live rock and cover bands, and dancing on the tables by midnight. Pure party energy.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the live bands and buzz, though some find it loud, packed and very touristy.
places.localTip: Go after 10pm when the band is in full swing, but expect a crush at the bar.
places.story: Wayne's has been the rambunctious live-music pub of old Nice for years, its small stage powering nightly rock and pop covers. By late evening the crowd is singing along and, often, dancing on the furniture. It's loud, lively and unapologetically fun.

places.name: High Club
places.reviewSummary: The biggest nightclub in Nice, a multi-room seafront venue with international DJs, a student night and a young, high-energy crowd. The city's main club.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the big-name DJs and energy, though they flag pricey drinks, queues and strict door policy.
places.localTip: Sunday's Studio 47 student night is the legendary one; dress smart to clear the door.
places.story: Right on the Promenade, the High Club packs several dance floors under one roof, from EDM headliners to its famous student nights. It's been the engine of Nice's clubbing scene for years. When the city wants to dance until dawn, this is where it goes.

places.name: Movida
places.reviewSummary: A lively seafront bar with two terraces over the water, creative tapas, cocktails and resident DJs. A favourite warm-up before the clubs.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the sea-view terraces and DJ nights, though tables go fast and it gets crowded late.
places.localTip: Grab a terrace table at sunset for the view, then stay as the DJ ramps it up.
places.story: Movida sits right on the Quai des États-Unis with the Mediterranean lapping below its terraces. Tapas and cocktails give way to dancing as the DJ takes over. It's the easy, sea-breeze start to a Nice night out.
places.highlight: Don't miss the upper terrace at golden hour.

places.name: Le Glam
places.reviewSummary: Nice's beloved LGBTQ+ club, all glitter, drag shows and high-energy DJ nights. The friendliest dance floor in town.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the inclusive, fun atmosphere and shows, though it's small and gets very packed after midnight.
places.localTip: Things really kick off after 1am; weekends bring the best drag and theme nights.
places.story: Le Glam has long been the rainbow heart of Nice nightlife, a sparkling cellar club where drag queens, glitter balls and pumping pop keep everyone dancing. The welcome is famously warm to all comers. It's where the city lets its hair fully down.
places.highlight: Don't miss the weekend drag shows.

places.name: Les Distilleries Idéales
places.reviewSummary: A beloved corner bar at a busy old-town crossroads, with a huge beer list, free nibbles at apéro and a perpetual buzz. A local classic.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the lively terrace, friendly prices and free snacks, though the corner gets very crowded at apéro.
places.localTip: Come for the 6-8pm apéro when generous free nibbles land with your drink.
places.story: On a five-street junction in the old town, Les Distilleries Idéales has become a ritual stop for after-work drinks. Its wood-panelled rooms and pavement crowd spill into the lanes nightly. Free olives and charcuterie at apéro make it a perennial favourite.

places.name: Le Comptoir Central Électrique
places.reviewSummary: A hip, industrial-chic cocktail and natural-wine bar in the buzzing port quarter, popular with a young local crowd. Effortlessly cool.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers praise the cocktails, vibe and DJ nights, though seating is limited and it fills early at weekends.
places.localTip: This is where Niçois warm up before clubbing; come on a Friday for the best atmosphere.
places.story: Housed in a former electrical depot in the up-and-coming port quarter, this bar helped make the area Nice's coolest after dark. Expect inventive cocktails, natural wines and a soundtrack from rotating DJs. It's the local alternative to the seafront tourist bars.

places.name: Le Bar des Oiseaux
places.reviewSummary: A characterful little old-town bar with art on the walls, good wine and occasional live music. An intimate, arty nightcap spot.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers enjoy the cosy, artsy feel and friendly staff, though the space is tiny and fills quickly.
places.localTip: Squeeze in for a late glass of wine after dinner in the old town's quiet lanes.
places.story: Tucked on a narrow Vieux Nice street, the Bar des Oiseaux carries a bohemian, lived-in charm with mismatched art and warm lighting. It's the kind of place locals slip into for one last glass. A gentle, soulful counter to the louder bars nearby.

places.name: Ma Nolan's Vieux Nice
places.reviewSummary: A long-running Irish pub near the Opera with live music, big-screen sport, quiz nights and a friendly international crowd. A reliable night out.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers like the live bands, sport and welcome, though it can be loud and crowded on match days.
places.localTip: Check the gig and match schedule; live bands play several nights a week.
places.story: Ma Nolan's has been the home of expats and travellers in Nice for years, pouring Guinness within sight of the Opera. Live bands, sport and pub quizzes give it a permanent buzz. It's the easy, English-speaking anchor of a Nice night.

places.name: La Part des Anges
places.reviewSummary: A natural-wine shop and bar with a serious bottle list and excellent charcuterie and cheese plates. A grown-up evening of good wine.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers praise the wine selection and knowledgeable staff, though tables are limited and it books up.
places.localTip: Buy a bottle off the shelf to drink in for a small corkage; the staff's pours are inspired.
places.story: La Part des Anges, named for the angels' share of wine that evaporates in the barrel, doubles as a cave and a wine bar. Its natural and biodynamic bottles draw a devoted local crowd. Settle in with cheese and a recommendation, and the evening glides by.

places.name: Molinard Perfume Workshop
places.reviewSummary: A historic Grasse perfume house with a Nice boutique offering create-your-own-perfume workshops. Blend and bottle your own scent.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the hands-on blending and take-home bottle, though the full workshops are pricey and need booking.
places.localTip: Reserve the perfume-creation session in advance; you leave with a personalised bottle and the formula.
places.story: Molinard has crafted fragrance in Grasse since 1849, and at its elegant Nice boutique you can sit at a perfumer's organ of essences. Guided by a 'nose', you blend your own scent from dozens of oils. It's a fragrant, hands-on souvenir of the Riviera's perfume heritage.
places.highlight: Don't miss leaving with your own bottled creation.

places.name: Cours Saleya Market Food Tour
places.reviewSummary: A guided tasting walk through the Cours Saleya market and old-town shops, sampling socca, olive oil, cheese and Niçoise specialities. Eat your way through the city.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests rave about the generous tastings and local knowledge, with the only caveat being a lot of walking and standing.
places.localTip: Skip breakfast; the tastings across the market and old town add up to a full meal.
places.story: These small-group tours wind through the Cours Saleya stalls and family-run shops, with a guide decoding chard tarts, candied fruit and Niçoise olive oil along the way. You taste as you go, meeting the vendors who supply the city. It's the most delicious crash course in local culture.

places.name: Poterie Augé-Laribé / Ceramic Studio
places.reviewSummary: Hands-on pottery and ceramics workshops in the old town, where you throw or hand-build your own Mediterranean-glazed piece. A creative afternoon.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the patient instruction and relaxed sessions, though class sizes are small so booking is essential.
places.localTip: Book ahead and choose a glaze in the warm Provençal colours the region is known for.
places.story: Provence has shaped clay for centuries, and Nice keeps the craft alive in small old-town studios where you can sit at a wheel for an afternoon. Patient teachers walk you from lump to glazed bowl. You leave with something made by your own hands and the region's earthy palette.

places.name: Bellet Wine Tasting (Vignobles de Nice)
places.reviewSummary: Tastings at the tiny Bellet appellation in the hills within Nice's own city limits, one of France's smallest and rarest wine areas. A hidden vineyard escape.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the rare wines and hillside views, though the estates are spread out and best reached by car.
places.localTip: Call ahead to arrange a visit; the family domaines are small and not always open to walk-ins.
places.story: Few realise Nice grows its own wine: the AOC Bellet, on terraced hills above the city, is one of France's smallest appellations, planted with rare local grapes. The rosés and whites barely leave the region. Sipping them where they're grown, with the sea hazy below, feels like a secret.

places.name: Castle Hill Panoramic Terrace
places.reviewSummary: The classic overhead shot of Nice: terracotta old-town roofs, the curving Baie des Anges and the blue chairs of the Promenade. The postcard view.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers call it the best view in Nice, though the climb and crowds at sunset come up often.
places.localTip: Arrive an hour before sunset for golden light on the old-town roofs and the bay turning pink.
places.story: From the Castle Hill terrace, the whole sweep of Nice lays itself out: ochre roofs tumbling to a curve of cobalt sea and the long white arc of the Promenade. Photographers have framed this exact scene for over a century. It never stops being the picture of the city.
places.highlight: Don't miss it just as the lamps flick on at dusk.

places.name: Place Rossetti
places.reviewSummary: The prettiest old-town square, framed by ochre and red façades, a splashing fountain and the cathedral dome. Endlessly photogenic.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the colours and lively café terraces, though it's busy and the terraces can be pricey.
places.localTip: Shoot from the fountain looking toward the cathedral; mornings have the softest light and fewer people.
places.story: Place Rossetti is the warm, glowing heart of old Nice, where ice-cream queues, café chairs and a baroque cathedral gather around a 19th-century fountain. The buildings blush in shades of red and gold. It's the square everyone photographs and nobody wants to leave.

places.name: Blue Chairs of the Promenade (Chaises Bleues)
places.address: Promenade des Anglais (near Jardin Albert 1er), 06000 Nice
places.reviewSummary: The famous azure chairs lining the Promenade, an enduring symbol of Nice against the sea and sky. A simple, iconic frame.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the symbolic shot and sea breeze, though the best chairs fill up fast in summer.
places.localTip: Frame a single empty chair against the blue sea for the city's signature minimalist photo.
places.story: The cobalt chairs of the Promenade des Anglais are so iconic they've become Nice's unofficial logo, repeated on postcards and posters. Generations have sat in them to watch the Baie des Anges shimmer. A single blue chair against blue water is the city in one frame.

places.name: Port Lympia Viewpoint
places.reviewSummary: The pastel-faced harbour of Nice, lined with yachts, fishing boats and neo-classical buildings. A colourful, less-crowded photo spot.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the colourful façades and boats, though some say the road around the port is busy with traffic.
places.localTip: Shoot from the Rauba Capeù bend above the port for boats, façades and the 'I love Nice' sign together.
places.story: Port Lympia, dug into the rock in the 1700s, is rimmed by tall Sardinian-era houses in apricot, rose and ochre, their reflections wobbling among the masts. From the cliff path above, the colours stack beautifully. It's the photogenic Nice that day-trippers often miss.
places.highlight: Don't miss the #ILoveNice sign on the Rauba Capeù headland.

places.name: Coulée Verte & Fontaine du Soleil
places.reviewSummary: The Promenade du Paillon, a green ribbon of fountains, mist jets and lawns linking the sea to the museums. Fun, reflective and very photogenic.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the mirror fountains and family atmosphere, though the water jets can be unpredictable.
places.localTip: Catch the mirror-pool fountains reflecting Place Masséna's red façades, and let kids cool off in the jets.
places.story: Where the covered Paillon river once ran, Nice laid out a glorious green park in 2013, full of mist fountains, reflecting pools and play areas. By day children dash through the water jets; at dusk the mirror pool doubles the city's red façades. It's the modern, joyful spine of central Nice.

places.name: Russian Cathedral Domes Viewpoint
places.address: Avenue Nicolas II / Boulevard du Tzaréwitch, 06000 Nice
places.reviewSummary: The six vivid onion domes of the Saint-Nicolas Russian cathedral rising against palm trees and blue sky. An unexpected, striking subject.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers find the domes stunning to photograph, though the surrounding streets are narrow and limit angles.
places.localTip: Shoot from Boulevard du Tzaréwitch in the morning to catch the domes lit and framed by palms.
places.story: Nothing prepares you for the riot of colour when the Saint-Nicolas cathedral's domes appear above a quiet Nice street, a fragment of imperial Russia under riviera palms. Built in 1912, they're the largest such ensemble outside Russia. Framed against blue sky and fronds, they make an unforgettable photo.
